titleStmt.title: The Ambassador in Korea (Muccio) to the Deputy Assistant Secretary of State for Far Eastern Affairs (Johnson)

head.hi: The Ambassador in Korea (Muccio) to the Deputy Assistant Secretary of State for Far Eastern Affairs (Johnson)

div.p: Dear Alex : I am enclosing for your personal background information a memorandum prepared by Phil Manhard commenting on the problem of the Chinese POWs on Koje-do. A few days back, I arranged for him to give similar impressions to General Yount in my presence in even more detail than he has in the enclosed memorandum. General Yount also wants Phil to talk directly to General Dodd, now in overall command on the island.

General Yount appreciates that there are many unsatisfactory aspects to the situation on the island. He does not have full confidence in the trustees and interpreters who are the go-betweens for the Americans and the Chinese prisoners themselves. He is now studying measures that might be more effective in making American policy and aims better known directly to the prisoners so as to make it more difficult for the interpreters or trustees to distort or shade the UN Command position.

head.hi: Memorandum by P. W. Manhard of the Political Section of the Embassy to the Ambassador in Korea (Muccio)

frus:attachment.p: The following comments are based on first-hand observation and knowledge gained incidental to interrogation work among Chinese POWs on Koje Island from July to December 1951 and during the last week of February 1952.

Chinese prisoners of war are controlled by a thin veneer of PW trustees not freely elected by the prisoners whom they control, but appointed by US Army camp authorities on the basis of ostensible anti-Communism. These trustees are mostly composed of two mutually antagonistic pro-Nationalist groups: (1) former CNA MP’s mostly captured by the CCF on Hainan Island and in South China, and (2) company-grade officer graduates of CNA military academies, many of whom were demoted to enlisted status upon absorption into the CCF. A third group of trustees, members of a secret society formerly strong in Ssuchuan Province, have been dominated by the first two groups in the camp. Very few members of any of these cliques ever engaged in serious combat against Chinese Communist forces during the warfare in China.

These trustees exercise discriminatory control over food, clothing, fuel and access to medical treatment for the mass of Chinese prisoners. With encouragement from Formosan Chinese assigned to PW work by GHQ Tokyo, the trustees have for several months conducted a drive to collect petitions for transfer to Formosa. This propaganda drive is now reaching a climax with use of brutal force to obtain signatures. The trustees maintain control over the Chinese compounds by means of force and coercion. Beatings, torture and threats of punishment are frequently utilized to intimidate the majority of Chinese POWs. Trustees have openly announced to fellow prisoners, “It has been officially decided that all who wish to do so will be guaranteed an opportunity to go to Formosa—those who do not wish to fight Communism will remain in the POW camp indefinitely.”

Recent, successful attempts to escape to other compounds by Chinese POWs reveal rising fear and alienation among ever larger numbers of Chinese prisoners from any possible pro-Nationalist attitude. If allowed freedom to choose a PW compound under the leadership of pro-Nationalists or pro-Communists, I believe many would flee their present compounds to pro-Communist ones in the hope of escaping intimidation and the denial of even minimum human freedoms by trustees whose behavior has commanded the respect of very few POWs.

Most of the prisoners believe that these trustees cannot remain in power without the support of the UN camp administrative authorities, and the mounting resentment among the Chinese POWs constitutes an increased threat to security within the UN POW camp.

This situation is vitiating efforts at reorientation of Chinese POWs, minimizing the appeal of the Chinese Nationalists, and playing into Communist hands by demonstrating an attempt at forced, coerced removal to Formosa in direct contradiction of the UNC stand at Panmunjom on voluntary repatriation of internees.
